---
title: "RMarkdown and knitr"
author: "Marc Los Huertos"
date: "EA030 · Compiled `r format(Sys.Date(), '%Y-%m-%d')`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    theme: null
    highlight: null
    toc: false
knit: (function(inputFile, encoding) {
    rmarkdown::render(
      inputFile,
      encoding = encoding,
      output_file = file.path(dirname(inputFile),
                              "../docs/RGuides/02_RMarkdown_knitr.html")
    )
  })
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 7,
  fig.height = 4
)
library(tibble)
library(knitr)
```

```{css, echo=FALSE}
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Libre+Baskerville:ital,wght@0,400;0,700;1,400&family=Source+Code+Pro:wght@400;600&family=Lato:wght@300;400;700&display=swap');

*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }

:root {
  --forest:    #1e3a2f;
  --moss:      #2d5a3d;
  --fern:      #4a7c5e;
  --sage:      #8aad96;
  --mist:      #d4e6da;
  --cream:     #f7f3ed;
  --parchment: #ede8df;
  --ink:       #1a1a18;
  --charcoal:  #3a3a38;
  --dust:      #7a7a72;
  --amber:     #c8832a;
  --amber-lt:  #e8a84a;
  --white:     #ffffff;
  --card-shadow: 0 2px 10px rgba(30,58,47,.09);
}

html { scroll-behavior: smooth; }
body {
  font-family: 'Lato', sans-serif;
  background: var(--cream);
  color: var(--ink);
  font-size: 17px;
  line-height: 1.75;
  padding-left:  clamp(14px, 4vw, 48px);
  padding-right: clamp(14px, 4vw, 48px);
  padding-bottom: 80px;
}
.main-container { max-width: 920px !important; margin: 0 auto !important;
                  padding: 0 clamp(10px, 2vw, 32px) 80px !important; }
.page-header {
  margin-left:  calc(-1 * clamp(14px, 4vw, 48px));
  margin-right: calc(-1 * clamp(14px, 4vw, 48px));
}
h1.title, h4.author, h4.date { display: none; }

.page-header {
  background-image:
    repeating-linear-gradient(120deg, transparent, transparent 60px,
      rgba(255,255,255,.018) 60px, rgba(255,255,255,.018) 61px),
    linear-gradient(160deg, #1e3a2f 0%, #2d5a3d 60%, #1a3d30 100%);
  color: #fff; padding: 44px 4vw 36px; margin: 0 -4vw 48px;
  border-bottom: 4px solid var(--amber); position: relative; overflow: hidden;
}
.page-header::after {
  content: ''; position: absolute; right: -40px; top: -40px;
  width: 260px; height: 260px; border-radius: 50%;
  background: radial-gradient(circle, rgba(138,173,150,.12) 0%, transparent 70%);
  pointer-events: none;
}
.page-header .module-tag {
  display: inline-block; font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace;
  font-size: .68rem; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: .18em; text-transform: uppercase;
  color: var(--amber-lt); border: 1px solid rgba(200,131,42,.45);
  padding: 3px 10px; border-radius: 2px; margin-bottom: 12px;
}
.page-header h1 {
  font-family: 'Libre Baskerville', serif; font-size: clamp(1.6rem, 3vw, 2.3rem);
  font-weight: 700; color: #fff; line-height: 1.2; margin-bottom: 8px;
}
.page-header h1 span { color: var(--sage); }
.page-header .sub { font-size: .9rem; color: rgba(255,255,255,.65); font-weight: 300; }
.page-header .sub strong { color: rgba(255,255,255,.9); font-weight: 700; }
.back-link {
  display: inline-block; margin-top: 16px;
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .7rem; letter-spacing: .08em;
  color: var(--amber-lt); text-decoration: none;
  border: 1px solid rgba(200,131,42,.4); padding: 4px 11px; border-radius: 3px;
  transition: background .2s;
}
.back-link:hover { background: rgba(200,131,42,.18); }

.section-nav {
  display: flex; gap: 8px; flex-wrap: wrap; margin-bottom: 44px;
  padding-bottom: 20px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--parchment);
}
.section-nav a {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .7rem; font-weight: 600;
  letter-spacing: .08em; text-transform: uppercase;
  color: var(--fern); text-decoration: none;
  border: 1px solid var(--mist); padding: 4px 12px; border-radius: 3px;
  background: var(--white); transition: background .2s, color .2s;
}
.section-nav a:hover { background: var(--mist); color: var(--forest); }

h2 {
  font-family: 'Libre Baskerville', serif; font-size: 1.45rem; font-weight: 700;
  color: var(--forest); margin-top: 56px; margin-bottom: 6px;
  padding-bottom: 8px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--parchment);
}
h3 {
  font-family: 'Libre Baskerville', serif; font-size: 1.1rem; font-weight: 700;
  color: var(--moss); margin-top: 28px; margin-bottom: 4px;
}
h4 {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .9rem; font-weight: 600;
  color: var(--charcoal); margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 4px;
  letter-spacing: .04em; text-transform: uppercase;
}

p { margin-bottom: 1em; }
strong { color: var(--forest); }
blockquote {
  border-left: 4px solid var(--sage); margin: 1.2em 0;
  padding: .5em 1em; background: var(--white);
  border-radius: 0 5px 5px 0; font-size: .94em; color: var(--charcoal);
}

code {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .84em;
  background: var(--mist); color: var(--moss); padding: 1px 5px; border-radius: 3px;
}
pre {
  background: var(--ink); color: #e8e6df; border-radius: 5px;
  padding: 14px 18px; font-size: .83rem; line-height: 1.55;
  overflow-x: auto; border-left: 4px solid var(--amber); margin: 1.2em 0;
}
pre code { background: transparent; color: inherit; padding: 0; font-size: 1em; }

.callout {
  border-left: 5px solid; padding: .65em 1.1em;
  margin: 1.4em 0; border-radius: 0 5px 5px 0; font-size: .92em; line-height: 1.6;
}
.callout > strong:first-child { display: block; margin-bottom: .3em; }
.callout-concept { border-color: var(--fern);  background: #f0f7f2; }
.callout-tip     { border-color: #2e7d32;      background: #f1f8f1; }
.callout-warn    { border-color: #e65100;      background: #fff8f4; }

/* ── Anatomy diagram ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
.anatomy {
  border: 2px solid var(--parchment); border-radius: 6px;
  overflow: hidden; margin: 1.6em 0; box-shadow: var(--card-shadow);
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .82rem; line-height: 1.6;
}
.anatomy-row {
  display: flex; align-items: stretch;
}
.anatomy-label {
  flex: 0 0 140px; background: var(--forest); color: var(--sage);
  font-size: .68rem; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: .12em;
  text-transform: uppercase; padding: 12px 14px;
  display: flex; align-items: center;
}
.anatomy-code {
  flex: 1; padding: 12px 16px; white-space: pre; overflow-x: auto;
}
.anatomy-row:nth-child(1) .anatomy-code { background: #1e2d3a; color: #e8c97a; }
.anatomy-row:nth-child(2) .anatomy-code { background: #1a2a1e; color: #a8d8b0; }
.anatomy-row:nth-child(3) .anatomy-code { background: #2a2210; color: #e8c97a; }
.anatomy-row:nth-child(4) .anatomy-code { background: var(--cream);  color: var(--ink);
                                           font-family: 'Lato', sans-serif;
                                           font-size: .88rem; }
.anatomy-divider { height: 2px; background: var(--parchment); }

/* ── Chunk option cards ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
.opt-grid {
  display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(220px, 1fr));
  gap: 10px; margin: 1.4em 0;
}
.opt-card {
  background: var(--white); border: 1px solid var(--mist);
  border-top: 3px solid var(--fern); border-radius: 4px;
  padding: 12px 14px; box-shadow: var(--card-shadow);
}
.opt-card .oc-name {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .85rem;
  font-weight: 600; color: var(--forest); margin-bottom: 3px;
}
.opt-card .oc-vals {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .73rem;
  color: var(--dust); margin-bottom: 6px;
}
.opt-card .oc-desc { font-size: .82rem; color: var(--charcoal); line-height: 1.45; }

/* ── Inline-R demo strip ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
.inline-demo {
  background: var(--white); border: 1px solid var(--mist);
  border-radius: 5px; padding: 14px 18px; margin: 1.2em 0;
  box-shadow: var(--card-shadow);
}
.inline-demo .id-source {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .82rem;
  background: var(--ink); color: #e8e6df; padding: 8px 12px;
  border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0; border-left: 4px solid var(--amber);
  white-space: pre-wrap; margin: 0 -18px 0 -18px;
}
.inline-demo .id-renders {
  font-size: .88rem; color: var(--charcoal); padding: 10px 0 0 0;
  border-top: 1px dashed var(--parchment); margin-top: 10px;
}
.inline-demo .id-label {
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .65rem;
  letter-spacing: .14em; text-transform: uppercase;
  color: var(--dust); margin-bottom: 4px;
}

table { font-size: .9rem; border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; margin: 1.2em 0; }
thead th {
  background: var(--forest); color: #fff;
  font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; font-size: .76rem;
  letter-spacing: .06em; text-transform: uppercase; padding: 8px 12px; text-align: left;
}
tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: var(--mist); }
tbody td { padding: 7px 12px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--parchment);
           vertical-align: top; }
tbody td:first-child { font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace;
                       color: var(--forest); font-weight: 600; white-space: nowrap; }

.key-box {
  background: var(--forest); color: #fff; border-radius: 6px;
  padding: 20px 24px; margin: 2em 0; font-size: .9rem;
}
.key-box h4 {
  font-family: 'Libre Baskerville', serif; color: var(--sage);
  font-size: 1rem; margin-bottom: 10px; text-transform: none; letter-spacing: 0;
}
.key-box ul { padding-left: 1.3em; }
.key-box li { margin-bottom: 5px; color: rgba(255,255,255,.85); }
.key-box code { background: rgba(255,255,255,.12); color: var(--amber-lt); }

.page-footer {
  background: var(--ink); color: rgba(255,255,255,.42); text-align: center;
  padding: 24px 4vw; margin: 60px -4vw 0;
  font-size: .76rem; font-family: 'Source Code Pro', monospace; letter-spacing: .04em;
}
.page-footer a { color: var(--sage); text-decoration: none; }
.page-footer .divider { margin: 0 8px; opacity: .3; }
```

<div class="page-header">
<div class="module-tag">EA030 &mdash; Core R Skills &mdash; Module 02</div>
<h1>RMarkdown <span>&amp; knitr</span></h1>
<p class="sub"><strong>Prof. Marc Los Huertos</strong> &nbsp;&bull;&nbsp; Pomona College</p>
<a class="back-link" href="../index.html">&#8592; Back to R Tricks</a>
</div>

<div class="section-nav">
<a href="#what-is-rmd">What is Rmd?</a>
<a href="#anatomy">Anatomy</a>
<a href="#yaml">YAML Header</a>
<a href="#markdown">Markdown Prose</a>
<a href="#chunks">Code Chunks</a>
<a href="#chunk-options">Chunk Options</a>
<a href="#inline">Inline R</a>
<a href="#setup-chunk">The Setup Chunk</a>
<a href="#images">Images</a>
<a href="#citations">Citations</a>
<a href="#rendering">Rendering</a>
<a href="#troubleshooting">Troubleshooting</a>
</div>

> **What this module covers.** An R Markdown file (`.Rmd`) weaves prose,
> code, and output into a single reproducible document. When you click
> **Knit**, knitr runs every chunk, captures its output, and pandoc
> converts everything to HTML, PDF, or Word. This module walks through
> every part of an Rmd file from scratch — YAML headers, markdown syntax,
> chunk options, inline R, and the rendering pipeline — so you understand
> not just *what* to write but *why* it works.

<hr>

## 1. What Is R Markdown? {#what-is-rmd}

A plain `.R` script runs code and prints results to the Console. A
`.Rmd` file does the same thing, but also lets you write formatted prose
*around* that code — headings, paragraphs, bullet lists, equations — and
produce a polished output document that a reader who has never opened
RStudio can read.

The three technologies involved are:

| Layer | Tool | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Text markup | **Markdown** | Headings, bold, lists, links, equations |
| Code execution | **knitr** | Runs R chunks, captures output and plots |
| Document conversion | **pandoc** | Converts the combined result to HTML/PDF/Word |

You never call pandoc or knitr directly. Clicking **Knit** (or running
`rmarkdown::render()`) triggers all three in sequence.

<div class="callout callout-tip">
<strong>💡 Create your first Rmd now</strong>
In RStudio: <strong>File → New File → R Markdown…</strong>. Choose HTML
output. RStudio generates a template with all three sections already present
— read it, then delete everything below the setup chunk and start fresh.
</div>

<hr>

## 2. The Anatomy of an Rmd File {#anatomy}

Every Rmd file has exactly three kinds of content, always in this order at
the top:

<div class="anatomy">
<div class="anatomy-row">
<div class="anatomy-label">YAML Header</div>
<div class="anatomy-code">---
title: "My Report"
author: "Marc Los Huertos"
output: html_document
---</div>
</div>
<div class="anatomy-divider"></div>
<div class="anatomy-row">
<div class="anatomy-label">Setup Chunk</div>
<div class="anatomy-code">```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE)
```</div>
</div>
<div class="anatomy-divider"></div>
<div class="anatomy-row">
<div class="anatomy-label">Code Chunk</div>
<div class="anatomy-code">```{r my-analysis}
x <- c(1, 4, 9, 16, 25)
mean(x)
```</div>
</div>
<div class="anatomy-divider"></div>
<div class="anatomy-row">
<div class="anatomy-label">Prose</div>
<div class="anatomy-code">## Results
<br>
The mean value was `r mean(c(1,4,9,16,25))`.
We collected data at **five** sites.</div>
</div>
</div>

These three elements — YAML, chunks, and prose — can appear in any order
after the header, interleaved as many times as needed. The YAML block must
be the very first thing in the file.

<hr>

## 3. The YAML Header {#yaml}

The YAML header controls the document's metadata and output format. It is
delimited by triple-dashes (`---`) and must appear at the very top of
the file, before any other content.

### Minimal header

<pre><code>---
title: "Air Quality Analysis"
author: "Marc Los Huertos"
date: "2024-03-15"
output: html_document
<hr>
</code></pre>

### Dynamic date

Hard-coding the date means it goes stale. Use inline R instead so the date
updates every time you knit:

<pre><code>date: "Compiled &#96;r format(Sys.Date(), '%Y-%m-%d')&#96;"
</code></pre>

### Output formats

The `output:` field determines what pandoc produces. The most common options:

| output value | Produces | Requires |
|---|---|---|
| `html_document` | Self-contained HTML file | nothing extra |
| `pdf_document` | PDF via LaTeX | a LaTeX installation (TinyTeX) |
| `word_document` | `.docx` Word file | nothing extra |
| `github_document` | Markdown for GitHub | nothing extra |

### HTML document options

<pre><code>output:
  html_document:
    toc: true              # table of contents
    toc_float: true        # floating sidebar TOC
    toc_depth: 3           # include h1, h2, h3 headings
    number_sections: true  # auto-number headings
    theme: flatly          # Bootswatch CSS theme
    highlight: tango       # syntax highlighting style
    self_contained: true   # embed all CSS/JS — one portable file
    df_print: paged        # paginated data frames
    code_folding: hide     # show "Code" buttons, hide by default
</code></pre>

<div class="callout callout-warn">
<strong>⚠️ YAML is whitespace-sensitive</strong>
Indentation in YAML is <em>exactly two spaces</em> — not tabs, not four
spaces. A misaligned line causes a cryptic parse error and the document
will not knit. If you see <code>Error in yaml::yaml.load()</code>, the
problem is almost always indentation or a missing colon.
</div>

### The custom `knit:` function

The modules in this course use a custom `knit:` field to redirect the
output HTML to a specific folder. This is what lets the knit button send
the file directly to the `docs/` folder without any manual copying:

<pre><code>knit: (function(inputFile, encoding) {
    rmarkdown::render(
      inputFile,
      encoding = encoding,
      output_file = file.path(dirname(inputFile),
                              "../docs/RGuides/02_RMarkdown_knitr.html")
    )
  })
</code></pre>

Without this, clicking Knit places the HTML in the same folder as the Rmd.
The `file.path(dirname(inputFile), "../docs/...")` pattern navigates
relative to the Rmd's location — so the path works on any machine
regardless of the absolute path to the project.

<div class="callout callout-concept">
<strong>📁 dirname() and relative paths</strong>
<code>dirname(inputFile)</code> returns the folder the Rmd lives in.
<code>"../docs/RGuides/..."</code> then navigates one level up (<code>..</code>)
and into <code>docs/RGuides/</code>. This means the Rmd and the docs folder
must be siblings — both children of the same project root — for the path to
resolve correctly.
</div>

<hr>

## 4. Markdown Prose {#markdown}

Between and around chunks, you write plain text with lightweight
**markdown** formatting. Pandoc converts this to HTML or whatever output
format you chose.

### Headings

```markdown
# Level 1 heading   (one #)
## Level 2 heading  (two ##)
### Level 3 heading (three ###)
```

Headings also create anchor links. Adding `{#my-id}` after the heading
text lets you link to it:

```markdown
## Results {#results}

See the [Results section](#results) for details.
```

### Emphasis, lists, and links

```markdown
**bold text**
*italic text*
`inline code` — use for function names, variable names, file paths

- unordered list item
- another item
  - nested item (two spaces of indent)

1. ordered list item
2. second item

[link text](https://example.com)
```

### Block quotes and horizontal rules

```markdown
> A blockquote — useful for key questions or callouts.

---    (three dashes = horizontal rule)
```

### Tables

```markdown
| Column A | Column B | Column C |
|----------|----------|----------|
| value 1  | value 2  | value 3  |
| value 4  | value 5  | value 6  |
```

The colons in the separator row control alignment:

```markdown
| Left     | Center   | Right    |
|:---------|:--------:|---------:|
| text     | text     | text     |
```

<div class="callout callout-tip">
<strong>💡 For data frames, use knitr::kable() instead</strong>
Never type a markdown table by hand for data. Use
<code>knitr::kable(df)</code> or <code>kableExtra</code> — it generates
the table automatically from your data so it updates when the data changes.
</div>

### Equations

R Markdown supports LaTeX math using `$` for inline and `$$` for display:

```markdown
The mean is $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$.

$$
\text{PM}_{2.5} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 T + \varepsilon
$$
```

Renders as:

The mean is $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$.

<hr>

## 5. Code Chunks {#chunks}

A code chunk is a fenced block that knitr executes. The output — printed
values, tables, plots — is inserted into the document immediately below
the chunk.

### Chunk anatomy

Every chunk has the same structure:

<pre><code>```{r chunk-label, option1 = value, option2 = value}
# your R code here
x <- 1 + 1
x
```
</code></pre>

- The opening fence is **exactly** `` ```{r `` — three backticks, open brace,
  the letter `r`, a space, then the label.
- The **label** is optional but strongly recommended. It appears in error
  messages and the knitr progress log, making debugging far easier.
- Labels must be **unique** across the entire document — duplicate labels
  cause an error at knit time.
- The closing fence is **exactly** `` ``` `` — three backticks, nothing else.

### Inserting a chunk

Three ways:

1. **Keyboard shortcut**: `Ctrl+Alt+I` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd+Option+I` (Mac)
2. **Toolbar**: the green **Insert** button → R
3. **Typing**: type the backtick fence manually

### What gets printed

Whatever R would print to the Console also appears in the document:

```{r chunk-output-demo}
# Expressions that print their result automatically
42
c(1, 2, 3)
summary(c(2.1, 3.4, 2.8, 4.1, 3.9))
```

Assignments do **not** print unless you explicitly print the object or wrap
in parentheses:

```{r assignment-printing}
x <- 10      # silent — nothing appears below this line
x            # now it prints
(y <- 20)    # wrapping assignment in () also prints
```

### Plots are output too

Any plot created inside a chunk is captured and embedded in the document:

```{r demo-plot, fig.cap="Particulate matter decreases with distance from the highway."}
distance_m  <- c(10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400)
pm25_ug_m3  <- c(38, 29, 21, 15,  11,   8)

plot(distance_m, pm25_ug_m3,
     xlab = "Distance from highway (m)",
     ylab = expression(PM[2.5] ~ (µg/m^3)),
     pch  = 19, col = "#4a7c5e",
     main = "PM2.5 vs Distance from Source")
lines(distance_m, pm25_ug_m3, col = "#4a7c5e", lwd = 1.5)
```

<hr>

## 6. Chunk Options {#chunk-options}

Chunk options control what knitr does with each chunk: whether to show
the code, whether to run it, how large to make the figure. They go inside
the `{r label, ...}` header, separated by commas.

### The essential options

<div class="opt-grid">
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">echo</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Show the source code in the output? <code>FALSE</code> hides code but still runs it and shows output.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">eval</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Run the chunk? <code>FALSE</code> shows code as a formatted block but does not execute it.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">include</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Include anything in the output? <code>FALSE</code> runs the chunk silently — code and output both hidden. Use for setup chunks.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">message</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Show <code>message()</code> output? Set <code>FALSE</code> to suppress library loading messages.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">warning</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Show warnings? Set <code>FALSE</code> to suppress, but check warnings interactively first — never hide problems you haven't understood.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">error</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Default <code>FALSE</code> means an error <em>stops the knit</em>. Set <code>TRUE</code> to show the error in the output and continue knitting — useful in teaching documents.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">fig.width</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">number (inches)</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Width of the output figure in inches. Default 7. Combine with <code>fig.height</code> to control aspect ratio.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">fig.height</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">number (inches)</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Height of the output figure in inches. Default 5.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">fig.cap</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">character string</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Figure caption. Appears below the plot. Supports inline R for dynamic captions.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">out.width</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">"100%", "70%", "400px"</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Width of the figure in the HTML output. Use <code>"100%"</code> to fill the column, or a percentage to shrink it.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">cache</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">TRUE / FALSE</div>
  <div class="oc-desc">Save chunk results and skip re-running if code hasn't changed. Useful for slow model fits. Use with care — can cause stale results.</div>
</div>
<div class="opt-card">
  <div class="oc-name">results</div>
  <div class="oc-vals">'markup', 'hide', 'asis', 'hold'</div>
  <div class="oc-desc"><code>'hide'</code> suppresses printed output. <code>'asis'</code> passes raw HTML/LaTeX through (for kable). <code>'hold'</code> collects all output and prints at the end of the chunk.</div>
</div>
</div>

### Common combinations

Rather than listing chunk headers in a code block (which knitr would try to
parse), here is a reference table of the most useful combinations:

| Goal | Options to set |
|---|---|
| Show code and output (default) | nothing — this is the default |
| Show output only, hide code | `echo = FALSE` |
| Show code only, don't run | `eval = FALSE` |
| Run silently, show nothing | `include = FALSE` |
| Show plot only, hide code and messages | `echo = FALSE, message = FALSE` |
| Show error in output without stopping knit | `error = TRUE` |
| Show figure with caption at a specific size | `fig.cap = "...", fig.width = 6, fig.height = 4` |

Each option goes inside the chunk header after the label, separated by commas:

```{r combo-example, eval=FALSE}
# Example: a results table — output only, no code shown
# ```{r results-table, echo=FALSE}

# Example: install instructions — show code but never run
# ```{r install-packages, eval=FALSE}

# Example: setup — run silently, nothing in output
# ```{r setup, include=FALSE}
```

### Seeing options in action

The next three chunks are identical code but with different options — compare
what appears in the rendered output:

```{r show-both, echo=TRUE}
# echo=TRUE (default) — you see this code AND the output below
sqrt(144)
```

```{r hide-code, echo=FALSE}
# echo=FALSE — output appears but code is hidden in the rendered document
sqrt(169)
```

```{r no-output, results='hide'}
# results='hide' — code appears, output is suppressed
sqrt(196)
```

### `fig.show` — controlling multi-plot chunks

When a chunk produces more than one plot, `fig.show` controls whether they
share a figure environment or each get their own:

<pre><code>```{r two-plots, fig.show='hold', fig.cap="Both plots side by side."}
# hold — both plots collected and placed together in one figure
hist(rnorm(200))
hist(rexp(200))
```

```{r two-plots-separate, fig.show='asis', fig.cap=c("First plot.", "Second plot.")}
# asis (default) — each plot placed immediately where it is produced
hist(rnorm(200))
hist(rexp(200))
```
</code></pre>

Note the `fig.cap` as a **character vector** — when `fig.show = 'asis'`
produces multiple figures from one chunk, supply a caption per plot:

```{r figcap-vector-demo, fig.show='hold', fig.width=3.5, fig.height=3,
    fig.cap=c("PM2.5 — roughly normal.", "PM10 — right-skewed.")}
set.seed(1)
hist(rnorm(300, mean = 12, sd = 4),
     main = "PM2.5", xlab = "µg/m³", col = "#4a7c5e", border = NA)
hist(rexp(300, rate = 0.08),
     main = "PM10",  xlab = "µg/m³", col = "#c8832a", border = NA)
```

### `ref.label` — reusing a chunk without repeating code

`ref.label` runs a previous chunk again without copying its code. This is
useful when you want to show code in one place and output in another, or
produce the same plot at two different sizes:

<pre><code>```{r my-plot, echo=TRUE, fig.show='hide'}
# Define and show the code here — suppress the figure with fig.show='hide'
plot(1:10, (1:10)^2, pch = 19, col = "#4a7c5e",
     xlab = "x", ylab = expression(x^2))
```

Some prose explaining the plot...

```{r my-plot-rendered, ref.label='my-plot', echo=FALSE, fig.cap="The actual figure."}
# Re-runs the my-plot chunk; echo=FALSE hides the code this time
```
</code></pre>

### `results = 'asis'` — passing raw HTML to the output

The default `results = 'markup'` wraps printed output in a code block.
`results = 'asis'` passes the output directly into the document as raw
HTML or markdown — required any time you generate tables or headings
programmatically:

```{r results-asis-demo, results='asis'}
# knitr::kable() generates an HTML table — needs results='asis' to render
# Without it, you'd see raw HTML tags as plain text
knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Site    = c("Kern", "Mojave", "Coast"),
    PM2.5   = c(12.1, 8.4, 18.0),
    n       = c(5L, 4L, 6L)
  ),
  caption = "Summary table generated inside a chunk."
)
```

<div class="callout callout-warn">
<strong>⚠️ kable() inside a for loop requires results='asis'</strong>
If you loop over groups and call <code>knitr::kable()</code> inside the loop,
you <em>must</em> set <code>results='asis'</code> on that chunk or the tables
will appear as raw HTML text. The same applies to any function that emits
raw HTML — <code>kableExtra</code>, <code>gt</code>, <code>DT::datatable()</code>.
</div>

<hr>

## 7. Inline R Code {#inline}

Inline R lets you embed a computed value directly in a sentence, so the
number updates automatically every time you knit. The syntax is a single
backtick, the letter `r`, a space, an R expression, and a closing backtick:

<pre><code>The mean PM2.5 was &#96;r round(mean(pm25_week), 1)&#96; µg/m³ across the study period.
</code></pre>

Anything that evaluates to a single printable value works: arithmetic,
function calls, object lookups, `format()` calls for formatting.

```{r inline-setup, echo=FALSE}
pm25_week  <- c(8.2, 11.4, 9.7, 42.3, 10.1, 9.3, 8.8)
n_sites    <- 7
start_date <- as.Date("2024-03-04")
end_date   <- as.Date("2024-03-10")
```

The source text below contains inline R — look at the rendered output:

**Source:**
```markdown
We monitored PM2.5 at `r n_sites` sites between
`r format(start_date, "%B %d")` and `r format(end_date, "%B %d, %Y")`.
The weekly mean was `r round(mean(pm25_week), 1)` µg/m³
(median: `r median(pm25_week)` µg/m³;
max: `r max(pm25_week)` µg/m³ on Thursday).
```

**Renders as:**

We monitored PM2.5 at `r n_sites` sites between
`r format(start_date, "%B %d")` and `r format(end_date, "%B %d, %Y")`.
The weekly mean was `r round(mean(pm25_week), 1)` µg/m³
(median: `r median(pm25_week)` µg/m³;
max: `r max(pm25_week)` µg/m³ on Thursday).

<div class="callout callout-concept">
<strong>🔗 Why inline R matters for reproducibility</strong>
If you type a number directly into prose — "the mean was 14.3 µg/m³" — and
later discover a data error and rerun the analysis, the number in the prose
stays wrong while your table updates. Inline R ensures the prose number is
always derived from the same computation as everything else.
</div>

### Formatting numbers for prose

```{r format-examples}
x <- 12345.6789

# Too many decimals for prose
x

# Rounded
round(x, 2)

# Formatted with thousands separator
format(x, big.mark = ",", nsmall = 2)

# Scientific notation suppressed
format(1.23e-5, scientific = FALSE, digits = 4)

# Percentage
paste0(round(0.734 * 100, 1), "%")
```

<div class="callout callout-warn">
<strong>⚠️ Keep inline R expressions short</strong>
Inline R is great for <code>round(mean(x), 1)</code>. It is not meant for
multi-step calculations. Compute your value in a chunk first, store it in
a named object, then reference that object inline. Debugging a broken
inline expression is much harder than debugging a broken chunk.
</div>

<hr>

## 8. The Setup Chunk {#setup-chunk}

Every Rmd should begin with a setup chunk immediately after the YAML header.
It does three things: sets global chunk options, loads all packages, and
optionally loads data.

<pre><code>```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# 1. Global chunk options — apply to every chunk unless overridden locally
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,     # show code by default
  warning = FALSE,    # suppress warnings globally
  message = FALSE,    # suppress package-load messages
  fig.width  = 7,     # default figure width (inches)
  fig.height = 4      # default figure height (inches)
)

# 2. Load all packages here — never scatter library() calls through the doc
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# 3. Optionally load data here so it is available to all later chunks
# df &lt;- read_csv("data/field_data.csv")
```
</code></pre>

The label `setup` is special — knitr always runs this chunk first,
regardless of where it appears in the file. The option `include=FALSE`
means none of this appears in the output: no code, no messages, no output.

### Why load all packages at the top?

If `library(ggplot2)` is inside chunk 7 and the document fails during knit
at chunk 5, you may spend time wondering why ggplot2 "isn't loaded" — when
the real error is earlier and ggplot2 never got a chance to load. A single
setup chunk makes the dependency list visible and ensures it runs first.

### Global vs local options

A local option on a single chunk **overrides** the global default from
`opts_chunk$set()`. Use this to make a specific chunk behave differently
without changing all chunks:

```{r override-demo, echo=FALSE, fig.width=4, fig.height=4}
# This chunk overrides the global echo=TRUE and fig.width/height
# echo=FALSE because this is a small illustrative plot, not teaching code
pie(c(Combustion = 52, Dust = 21, Secondary = 18, Other = 9),
    col = c("#1e3a2f","#4a7c5e","#c8832a","#8aad96"),
    main = "Typical PM2.5 Source Contribution")
```

<hr>

## 9. Images {#images}

There are two ways to embed an image: the plain markdown syntax for simple
cases, and `knitr::include_graphics()` for anything that needs chunk options
(sizing, captions, cross-references).

### Markdown syntax

```markdown
![Alt text describing the image](path/to/image.png)

![Caption shown below the image](figures/site_map.png){width=70%}
```

The path is relative to the Rmd file's location. `{width=70%}` is a
pandoc attribute that scales the image in HTML output. This approach is
fine for decorative images but gives you no chunk-level control.

### `knitr::include_graphics()` — the preferred approach

<pre><code>```{r site-map, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Sensor deployment locations, Spring 2024.",
    out.width="80%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/site_map.png")
```
</code></pre>

Use `out.width` (not `fig.width`) for images loaded from disk — `fig.width`
controls the size of *generated* R plots; `out.width` controls the display
size of any figure in the output HTML.

### Finding and specifying the path

The path is always **relative to the Rmd file**. If your project looks like:

```
project/
  reports/
    my_analysis.Rmd      ← Rmd lives here
  figures/
    site_map.png
  data/
    field_data.csv
```

Then the correct path inside the Rmd is `"../figures/site_map.png"` — one
level up (`..`), then into `figures/`. The same logic applies to data files.

```{r image-path-example, eval=FALSE}
# Rmd is in reports/; figure is in figures/ at the project root
knitr::include_graphics("../figures/site_map.png")

# If figures/ is a subfolder of the same folder as the Rmd:
knitr::include_graphics("figures/site_map.png")
```

### Supported formats

`knitr::include_graphics()` accepts `.png`, `.jpg`/`.jpeg`, `.gif`, and
`.svg` for HTML output. For PDF output, use `.png` or `.pdf` — `.gif` and
`.svg` are not supported by LaTeX.

<div class="callout callout-tip">
<strong>💡 Screenshots and field photos</strong>
For screenshots (e.g. of RStudio, an instrument interface, a map): save as
`.png` (not `.jpg` — JPEG compression blurs text). Aim for 150–200 dpi at
the intended display size. For field photos where file size matters,
<code>.jpg</code> is fine.
</div>

<hr>

## 10. Citations and Bibliography {#citations}

R Markdown uses **pandoc-citeproc** to manage citations. You provide a
`.bib` file (BibTeX format) and reference entries inline with `@key`
syntax. Pandoc assembles the bibliography automatically at the end of
the document.

### Step 1 — Create a `.bib` file

A `.bib` file is a plain-text file containing reference entries. Each
entry has a type (`@article`, `@book`, etc.), a cite key you invent, and
fields:

```bibtex
@article{smith2022pm25,
  author  = {Smith, Jane and Jones, Robert},
  title   = {Particulate Matter Trends in Southern California},
  journal = {Environmental Science \& Technology},
  year    = {2022},
  volume  = {56},
  number  = {4},
  pages   = {2341--2350},
  doi     = {10.1021/acs.est.1c07890}
}

@book{wickham2023r4ds,
  author    = {Wickham, Hadley and Cetinkaya-Rundel, Mine and Grolemund, Garrett},
  title     = {R for Data Science},
  edition   = {2},
  year      = {2023},
  publisher = {O'Reilly Media},
  url       = {https://r4ds.hadley.nz}
}

@manual{r-base,
  title        = {R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing},
  author       = {{R Core Team}},
  organization = {R Foundation for Statistical Computing},
  address      = {Vienna, Austria},
  year         = {2024},
  url          = {https://www.R-project.org/}
}
```

Save this as `references.bib` in the same folder as your Rmd (or a
`bib/` subfolder — adjust the path in YAML accordingly).

<div class="callout callout-tip">
<strong>💡 Getting BibTeX entries without typing them</strong>
Most journal websites have a "Cite" or "Export citation" button — choose
BibTeX format. Google Scholar has a quotation-mark icon → BibTeX.
Zotero and Mendeley both export <code>.bib</code> files directly. The only
thing you need to edit is the cite key (the first field after the opening
brace) — make it memorable: <code>smith2022pm25</code>, not <code>smith2022a</code>.
</div>

### Step 2 — Add `bibliography:` to the YAML header

<pre><code>---
title: "Air Quality Analysis"
author: "Marc Los Huertos"
output: html_document
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl               # optional — citation style (APA, MLA, etc.)
link-citations: true       # make in-text citations clickable links
<hr>
</code></pre>

Without `csl:`, pandoc uses its built-in Chicago author-date style.
Download `.csl` files for other styles from the
[Zotero Style Repository](https://www.zotero.org/styles).

### Step 3 — Cite in your prose

```markdown
Particulate matter exposure is linked to cardiovascular disease [@smith2022pm25].

Smith and Jones [-@smith2022pm25] found that PM2.5 declined 40% over two decades.

For an introduction to R, see @wickham2023r4ds [p. 42].

Multiple citations: [@smith2022pm25; @r-base].
```

| Syntax | Renders as |
|---|---|
| `[@key]` | (Smith and Jones 2022) |
| `[-@key]` | (2022) — suppress author name |
| `@key` | Smith and Jones (2022) — narrative citation |
| `[@key, p. 42]` | (Smith and Jones 2022, p. 42) |
| `[@key1; @key2]` | (Smith 2022; Jones 2021) |

### Step 4 — The bibliography appears automatically

Pandoc places the formatted reference list at the **end of the document**
by default. To control placement — for example, before a footer — insert
a heading with the `{#refs}` div exactly where you want it:

```markdown
## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<hr>
*Compiled on `r Sys.Date()`.*
```

### Citing R packages

It is good practice to cite the R packages you use. `knitr::write_bib()`
generates BibTeX entries for any installed packages automatically:

```{r write-bib, eval=FALSE}
# Run once to generate/update the bib file for the packages you use
knitr::write_bib(
  c("base", "rmarkdown", "knitr", "tidyverse", "lubridate", "ggplot2"),
  file = "references.bib"
)
```

Then cite as normal: `[@R-ggplot2]`, `[@R-tidyverse]`. The `R-` prefix is
the key format `write_bib()` produces.

<div class="callout callout-concept">
<strong>📚 nocite — bibliography entry with no in-text citation</strong>
To list a source in the bibliography without citing it in the prose — a
"Further reading" entry — add a <code>nocite</code> field to the YAML header:

<pre>nocite: |
  @wickham2023r4ds, @r-base</pre>

These appear in the reference list even though no <code>@key</code> appears
in the body text.
</div>

<hr>

## 11. Rendering the Document {#rendering}

### The Knit button

The simplest way to render is the **Knit** button in the RStudio toolbar
(the yarn-and-needle icon). It calls `rmarkdown::render()` on the current
file, runs all chunks in a **fresh R session**, and opens the output in the
Viewer pane.

### From the Console

```{r render-console, eval=FALSE}
# Render to the default output format specified in YAML
rmarkdown::render("reports/my_analysis.Rmd")

# Render to a specific format regardless of YAML
rmarkdown::render("reports/my_analysis.Rmd",
                  output_format = "pdf_document")

# Render and save to a specific path
rmarkdown::render("reports/my_analysis.Rmd",
                  output_file  = "outputs/my_analysis.html")
```

### What "fresh R session" means — and why it matters

When you click Knit, RStudio starts a **new, empty R session** to run your
document. It does not use the objects already in your Environment pane.
This is the most common source of "it works in the Console but fails to
knit" confusion:

<div class="callout callout-warn">
<strong>⚠️ Your Environment is not your document's environment</strong>
If you ran <code>df &lt;- read_csv(...)</code> in the Console and forgot to
put it in a chunk, <code>df</code> exists in your environment but not in
the knit session. The document will error with <code>object 'df' not found</code>.
The fix: every object the document needs must be created by a chunk <em>inside
the document</em>.
</div>

### Rendering order

knitr executes chunks **strictly top to bottom**. An object created in
chunk 5 is not available in chunk 3, even if you created it in the Console
first. If you get `object not found`, check whether the object is created
*before* it is used.

```{r chunk-order-demo}
# This chunk creates the object
site_data <- tibble::tibble(
  site    = c("Kern", "Mojave", "Coast"),
  pm25    = c(12.1, 8.4, 18.0)
)
```

```{r chunk-uses-object}
# This chunk uses it — works because it comes AFTER the chunk that creates it
site_data
```

### Render vs Source vs Run All

| Action | R session | Chunks run | Use for |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knit** | Fresh, empty | All, top to bottom | Final output, submission |
| **Run All** (Ctrl+Alt+R) | Current session | All, top to bottom | Development — your objects are available |
| **Run chunk** (Ctrl+Shift+Enter) | Current session | Just this chunk | Iterative development |
| **Source** on a `.R` file | Current session | — | Running a plain R script |

<div class="callout callout-tip">
<strong>💡 Development workflow</strong>
While building a document, use <strong>Run chunk</strong> to iterate quickly
(you have access to your environment). Before submitting or sharing, always
do a full <strong>Knit</strong> from scratch to confirm the document is truly
self-contained.
</div>

### Knit errors — reading the error output

When a knit fails, RStudio shows the error in the R Markdown tab. The
format is always:

```
Quitting from R02_RMarkdown_knitr.Rmd:45-52 [chunk-label]
Error in ... : error message here
```

The numbers `45-52` are the **line numbers** where the failing chunk
starts and ends. Go to that line in your Rmd to find the problem. The
`[chunk-label]` confirms which chunk it was — another reason to always
label your chunks.

<hr>

## 12. Troubleshooting Common Problems {#troubleshooting}

### Checklist for "it runs but won't knit"

```{r troubleshoot-checklist, eval=FALSE}
# 1. Is every object the document needs created INSIDE a chunk?
#    (Not just in your Console environment)

# 2. Are all packages loaded with library() inside a chunk?
#    (library() calls in the Console do nothing for the knit session)

# 3. Is every file path correct relative to the Rmd's location?
#    (The knit session's working directory is the folder the Rmd lives in)
getwd()   # run in Console to see where your Rmd's working directory is

# 4. Are all chunk labels unique?
#    (Duplicate labels cause an immediate error before any code runs)

# 5. Does the chunk header use commas between options, not semicolons?
#    CORRECT:  ```{r my-chunk, echo=FALSE, fig.width=6}
#    WRONG:    ```{r my-chunk; echo=FALSE; fig.width=6}
```

### Working directory in the knit session

The knit session's working directory is the **folder the Rmd lives in** —
not your RStudio project root, and not wherever you last called `setwd()`.
This means all relative file paths in `read_csv()`, `source()`, etc. should
be relative to the Rmd file, not to the project root.

```{r wd-demo, eval=FALSE}
# If your Rmd is at:  project/reports/my_analysis.Rmd
# And your data is at: project/data/field_data.csv
# Then the correct relative path in the Rmd is:
df <- read_csv("../data/field_data.csv")   # one level up, then into data/

# NOT:
df <- read_csv("data/field_data.csv")      # wrong — that looks for
                                           # project/reports/data/ which doesn't exist
```

### YAML parse errors

```
Error in yaml::yaml.load(string, ...) :
  Scanner error: ...
```

This means the YAML header is malformed. Common causes:

- **Tabs instead of spaces** — YAML requires spaces only
- **Missing colon** after a key name
- **Special characters** in the title not wrapped in quotes: `title: My: Report` fails; `title: "My: Report"` works
- **Mismatched quotes** — if your title contains a single quote (apostrophe), use double quotes around it
- **Wrong indentation** — nested fields must be exactly 2 spaces deeper than their parent

### Chunk delimiter errors

```
Error: attempt to use zero-length variable name
```
or
```
Unterminated chunk
```

These usually mean a chunk fence is wrong:

```markdown
CORRECT opening:  ```{r my-label}
WRONG — missing r: ```{my-label}
WRONG — extra space: ``` {r my-label}

CORRECT closing:  ```
WRONG — has text:  ``` r
WRONG — two ticks: ``
```

### Package not available

```
Error in library(ggplot2) : there is no package called 'ggplot2'
```

The knit session installs nothing automatically. Install missing packages
in the Console with `install.packages("ggplot2")`, then knit again.
Never put `install.packages()` in a chunk that will be evaluated during
knit — it re-installs the package every time.

<hr>

<div class="key-box">
<h4>🔑 RMarkdown and knitr — key takeaways</h4>
<ul>
<li><strong>Three parts, one file.</strong> YAML header (metadata and format), prose (markdown), and code chunks (R). They interleave freely; the YAML block must come first.</li>
<li><strong>Knit runs a fresh session.</strong> Every object and package must be created inside the document. Objects in your Environment pane do not exist during knit.</li>
<li><strong>Chunks run top to bottom.</strong> An object created in chunk 7 cannot be used in chunk 3. Read errors as line numbers and chunk labels.</li>
<li><strong>The setup chunk is special.</strong> Label it <code>setup</code>, use <code>include=FALSE</code>, load all packages and set global options here. knitr always runs it first.</li>
<li><strong>Local options override global ones.</strong> Set sensible defaults in <code>opts_chunk$set()</code> and override per-chunk only when needed.</li>
<li><strong><code>fig.show='hold'</code> collects plots; <code>'asis'</code> places each immediately.</strong> Use a character vector for <code>fig.cap</code> when one chunk produces multiple figures. Use <code>ref.label=</code> to reuse a chunk without repeating code.</li>
<li><strong><code>results='asis'</code> is required for programmatic tables.</strong> Any function that emits raw HTML — <code>knitr::kable()</code>, <code>kableExtra</code>, <code>gt</code> — needs this option or its output appears as raw text.</li>
<li><strong>Inline R keeps prose in sync.</strong> Write <code>`r round(mean(x), 1)`</code> in prose instead of typing a number. The value updates automatically when data changes.</li>
<li><strong>Use <code>knitr::include_graphics()</code> for images.</strong> It respects chunk options (<code>out.width</code>, <code>fig.cap</code>, <code>fig.align</code>). Paths are relative to the Rmd file, not the project root.</li>
<li><strong>Citations need a <code>.bib</code> file and a YAML <code>bibliography:</code> field.</strong> Cite with <code>[@key]</code> in prose; use <code>knitr::write_bib()</code> to generate entries for R packages automatically.</li>
<li><strong>YAML is whitespace-sensitive.</strong> Two spaces for indentation, no tabs, colons after every key. Quote any value that contains special characters.</li>
<li><strong>Working directory = Rmd folder.</strong> All relative paths in <code>read_csv()</code>, <code>knitr::include_graphics()</code>, and <code>bibliography:</code> are relative to the Rmd file's location.</li>
</ul>
</div>

<hr>

### What's next?

- **[Data Import](03_Data_Import.html)** — reading CSV, Excel, and field-data files into R
- **[Data Wrangling](04_Data_Wrangling.html)** — cleaning and reshaping with `dplyr` and `tidyr`
- **[Visualization](05_Visualization.html)** — `ggplot2` for publication-quality figures

```{r session-info, echo=FALSE}
# sessionInfo()
```

<footer class="page-footer">
EA030 Core R Skills &mdash; Module 02
<span class="divider">|</span> Prof. Marc Los Huertos
<span class="divider">|</span> Pomona College
<span class="divider">|</span> Last updated: `r format(Sys.Date(), "%B %Y")`
<span class="divider">|</span> <a href="https://github.com/marclos/RTricks">GitHub</a>
</footer>
